verses.commentary: Jesus continues in parables. The third parable in this confrontation cycle (with two sons, wicked tenants, now wedding feast).
Kingdom like a king who gave a wedding banquet for his son. Royal wedding imagery, the highest social event. Messianic banquet (Isaiah 25:6) framed as a wedding.
Sends servants to call the invited (kekleminous, κεκλημένους — those already pre-invited). They refuse. The first refusal.
Sends more servants — 'My dinner is ready, oxen and fatted calves are killed, everything is ready. Come.' The provision is lavish; the menu announced; the call is reasonable.
They make light of it — one to his farm, another to his business. Mundane preference over royal invitation. Dismissive indifference.
The rest seize, mistreat, and kill the servants. Active hostility from a subset. Echo of 21:35-36 — the prophets' fate again.
King angered; sends troops, destroys those murderers, burns their city. Often read as foreshadowing of Jerusalem's destruction in AD 70.
'Wedding feast is ready, but those invited were not worthy.' The unworthiness was their refusal. The status of being invited did not survive their response.
'Go to the highways and invite whoever you find.' Universal invitation now goes out. Boundaries dissolve.
Servants gather all — both good and bad. Wedding hall fills with guests. Inclusive net (compare 13:47-48). The wedding does not stay vacant.
King enters; sees a man without wedding garment. Custom of host providing garments; this man refused or was inappropriate.
'Friend (hetaire, ἑταῖρε — same neutral-cool address as 20:13), how did you get in without a wedding garment?' Man is silent — no defense.

Bound hand and foot, thrown into outer darkness — weeping and gnashing of teeth. Inclusion in the wedding does not exempt from accountability for response. Grace is universal in invitation; particular in transformation.
'Many are called, few are chosen.' Klesis (κλῆσις, calling) and eklogē (ἐκλογή, election) distinguished. The invitation widens; the actual reception narrows.
Pharisees plot how to entangle (pagideuso, παγιδεύσωσιν) Jesus in his speech. Hostile word-trap.

Send disciples plus Herodians (political party loyal to Herod, normally Pharisees' opponents). Strange alliance signals desperation. Flattery: 'You are true, teach God's way in truth, courting no one's favor.' Sets up the trap.
'Is it lawful to pay taxes to Caesar or not?' Lose-lose: yes = pro-Roman collaboration (alienate the crowd); no = sedition (Roman charge).
Jesus knows their malice (poneria, πονηρία): 'Hypocrites, why do you test me?'
'Show me the coin for the tax.' Denarius produced — itself loaded with idolatrous imagery (Caesar's image, divine inscription).
'Whose likeness (eikon, εἰκών) and inscription is this?' Setting up the punchline.

'Caesar's.' 'Render to Caesar what is Caesar's, and to God what is God's.' Brilliant evasion: pay him in his own coin if you wish; render to God his image — yourselves (Genesis 1:27). Dual-sovereignty logic.
They marveled (ethaumasan, ἐθαύμασαν) and left him. Trap defused.
Same day — Sadducees (priestly aristocracy denying resurrection) try a different angle.
Cite Deuteronomy 25:5 — levirate marriage (yibbum). Brother marries the widow to raise an heir.
Seven brothers in succession marry the same woman — each dies. Constructed hypothetical, designed to be absurd.
Through all seven brothers.
Last of all, the woman dies. The setup is complete.
'In the resurrection, whose wife will she be of the seven?' The 'gotcha' — resurrection reduced to absurdity by polyandry math.
'You are wrong — you know neither the Scriptures nor the power of God.' Twin diagnosis: textual ignorance + theological inadequacy.
Resurrection: neither marry nor are given in marriage. Like angels. The eschatological state transforms — earthly institutions are not simply continued.
Re-direct to scripture: 'Have you not read what was spoken by God?' Scripture is the arena of dispute.

Exodus 3:6 quoted: 'I am the God of Abraham, Isaac, Jacob.' Present tense — 'I am.' God is not God of the dead but of the living. The patriarchs must live; therefore resurrection. Tight exegesis from Torah they accept.
Crowds astonished. Theological skill recognized by the people if not by the questioners.
Pharisees hearing the Sadducees silenced regroup. Round three.
A lawyer (nomikos, νομικός) tests him — third round of testing in this chapter.
'Which is the great commandment in the Law?' Standard rabbinic question — how to prioritize within 613 mitzvot.
Citation of Deuteronomy 6:5 — heart, soul, mind (the Shema with 'mind' added). Total love of God.
'This is the great and first commandment.' Vertical love anchors all.
'The second is like it' — Leviticus 19:18 — 'love your neighbor as yourself.' Horizontal love coupled. They are 'like' in priority and character.
'All the Law and the Prophets depend (kremantai, κρέμανται, hang) on these two.' Torah and prophetic books are coherent around love. Hermeneutical center named.
Jesus takes the offensive. Pharisees gathered.
'What do you think of the Christ? Whose son is he?' 'David's,' they answer — standard Jewish messianology.
'How is it then that David, in the Spirit, calls him Lord?' Citation incoming.
Psalm 110:1: 'The Lord said to my Lord, Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool.' YHWH and David's Lord distinguished. The Messiah is more than David's biological descendant.
'If David calls him Lord, how is he his son?' Christological puzzle: Messiah is David's son AND David's Lord. Resolves only in Jesus' divine-human identity.

No one could answer a word. From that day, no one dared ask him any more questions. The cross-examinations end with Jesus' counter-examination unanswered. The discourse battle is won; the political battle is just beginning.
